coredata.dc:title: Hemorheological characteristics of red blood cells exposed to surface functionalized graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Kim, Jeongho
Nafiujjaman, Md
Nurunnabi, Md
Lee, Yong-Kyu
Park, Hun-Kuk
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are potential candidates for various biomedical applications such as drug delivery, bioimaging, cell labeling, and biosensors. However, toxicological information on their effects on red blood cells (RBCs) and the mechanisms involved remain unexplored. To the best of our knowledge, our study is the first to investigate the toxicity effects of three GQDs with different surface functionalizations on the hemorheological characteristics of human RBCs, including hemolysis, deformability, aggregation, and morphological changes. RBCs were exposed to three different forms of GQDs (non-functionalized, hydroxylated, and carboxylated GQDs) at various concentrations (0, 500, 750, and 1000 μg/mL) and incubation times (0, 1, 2, 3, or 4 h). The rheological characteristics of the RBCs were measured using microfluidic-laser diffractometry and aggregometry. Overall, the hemolysis rate and rheological alterations of the RBCs were insignificant at a concentration less than 500 μg/mL. Carboxylated GQDs were observed to have more substantial hemolytic activity and caused abrupt changes in the deformability and aggregation of the RBCs than the non-functionalized or hydroxylated GQDs at concentrations >750 μg/mL. Our findings indicate that hemorheological assessments could be utilized to estimate the degree of toxicity to cells and to obtain useful information on safety sheets for nanomaterials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Toxicology
Graphene quantum dot
Red blood cell
Haemolysis
Aggregation
Deformability

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of blood samples and GQD toxicity assessment

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–Vis spectroscopy and photoluminescence spectroscopy of the non-functionalized (A, B), hydroxylated (C, D), and carboxylated GQDs (E, F).

Hemoglobin release from the RBCs exposed to three different graphene quantum dot (GQD) nanomaterials (NMs), measured using a spectrophotometer after 2-h exposure. Mean values ± SD are given, n = 3. * denotes p < 0.05.

The diffraction images from a diffraction pattern of RBCs obtained using a charge-coupled device (CCD) video camera at a shear stress of 3 Pa after 3-h exposure of graphene quantum dot (GQD) nanomaterials at a concentration of (A) 500 μg/mL and (B) 750 μg/mL. The left, middle, and right images indicate RBCs exposed to non-functionalized, hydroxylated, and carboxylated GQDs, respectively, in each column.

(A) Changes in the deformability of the RBCs exposed to GQD nanomaterials at different concentrations (0, 500, and 750 μg/mL) at room temperature for 3 h. (B) Changes in the deformability of the RBCs exposed to GQDs for different incubation times (0, 1, 2, and 3 h) at a concentration of 750 μg/mL at room temperature. Mean values ± SD are given, n = 3.

(A) Changes in the aggregation of the RBCs exposed to GQD nanomaterials at different concentrations (0, 500, and 750 μg/mL) at room temperature for 3 h. (B) Changes in the aggregation of the RBCs exposed to the GQDs at room temperature for different incubation times (0, 1, 2, and 3 h) at a concentration of 750 μg/mL. Mean values ± SD are given, n = 3. * denotes p < 0.05.

Field emission scanning electron microscopic images of the RBCs exposed to three graphene quantum dots (GQDs) at a concentration of 750 μg/mL for 3 h. (A) Control (B) Hydroxylated GQDs (C) Carboxylated GQDs (D) non-functionalized GQDs. SEM images were taken at 1000×, 5000×, 10000×, and 20000× magnifications. Scale bars in inset pictures represent 50 μm, 10 μm, 5 μm, and 2 μm, respectively.
Comparison of hydrodynamic size for three different forms of GQDs (non-functionalized, hydroxylated, and carboxylated GQDs) at various concentrations measured by dynamic light scattering.

head.ce:title: Hemorheological characteristics of red blood cells exposed to surface functionalized graphene quantum dots

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Biomedical Engineering, College of Medicine, Kyung Hee University, 26 Kyungheedae-ro, Dongdaemun-gu, Seoul 02447, Republic of Korea. Tel.: +82 2 961 0290

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Biomedical Engineering, College of Medicine

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Green Bioengineering, Korea National University of Transportation, Daehak-ro, Chungju-si, Chunbuk 380-702, Republic of Korea. Tel.: +82 43 841 5224.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are potential candidates for various biomedical applications such as drug delivery, bioimaging, cell labeling, and biosensors. However, toxicological information on their effects on red blood cells (RBCs) and the mechanisms involved remain unexplored. To the best of our knowledge, our study is the first to investigate the toxicity effects of three GQDs with different surface functionalizations on the hemorheological characteristics of human RBCs, including hemolysis, deformability, aggregation, and morphological changes. RBCs were exposed to three different forms of GQDs (non-functionalized, hydroxylated, and carboxylated GQDs) at various concentrations (0, 500, 750, and 1000 μg/mL) and incubation times (0, 1, 2, 3, or 4 h). The rheological characteristics of the RBCs were measured using microfluidic-laser diffractometry and aggregometry. Overall, the hemolysis rate and rheological alterations of the RBCs were insignificant at a concentration less than 500 μg/mL. Carboxylated GQDs were observed to have more substantial hemolytic activity and caused abrupt changes in the deformability and aggregation of the RBCs than the non-functionalized or hydroxylated GQDs at concentrations >750 μg/mL. Our findings indicate that hemorheological assessments could be utilized to estimate the degree of toxicity to cells and to obtain useful information on safety sheets for nanomaterials.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have drawn a lot of research interest in the biomedical fields owing to their superiority over conventional semiconductor-based quantum dots; their advantages include low cost, high chemical stability, high photostability, low toxicity, and excellent biocompatibility (Geim, 2009; Shen et al., 2012; Wen et al., 2015). GQDs have been investigated for many different applications such as photovoltaic devices (Gupta et al., 2011), biosensing (Ran et al., 2013), cellular imaging (Sun et al., 2015), drug delivery, and cancer therapy (Ge et al., 2015).

For using GQDs in biomedical applications, the main criteria are their toxicity profiles. Literature reviews have reported that nano-sized GQDs could be internalized into cells quicker than micro-sized graphene oxide nanomaterials. Nurunnabi et al. showed that the GQDs showed no acute toxicity and did not cause any morphological changes in different types of cancer cells and their in vivo study revealed that the GQDs did not cause significant toxicity to the treated animals (Nurunnabi et al., 2013c). However, it should be noted that most of the reported cytotoxicity studies are based on MTT assays, and very few systematic cytotoxicity evaluations have been conducted for assessing the biocompatibility of GQDs. Xu and his group reported that different types of GQD internalizations did not induce significant cytotoxicity, as proved by the high viability, low LDH release, low internal cellular ROS levels, and negligible amount of cell apoptosis (Jiang et al., 2015). In addition, extended use of GQD-based nanotechnology has resulted in concerns regarding the risk of overexposure in humans (Yokel and MacPhail, 2011). In fact, a recent epidemiological study showed a high correlation between exposure to nanoparticles and the incidence of life threatening cardiovascular events (Buzea et al., 2007). Blood, which directly or indirectly encounters any hazardous materials, is adept to transport foreign substances to cells, tissues, and organs. In this case, it is very important to study the toxicity of GQDs on blood, particularly erythrocytes (Li et al., 2008). Accidental exposure by different routes of administration can potentially lead to the translocation of nanomaterials into the bloodstream (Smock et al., 2014). In particular, many of the proposed biomedical applications require direct injection of nanomaterials through different routes of administration, which will most probably also lead to direct contact of the nanomaterials with blood components. Therefore, evaluation of GQD biocompatibility with blood is a necessary part of early development of nanotechnology-derived medical devices and drug carriers.

Several studies have been reported on the toxicity of graphene on red blood cells (RBCs) by measuring hemolysis, but the deformability and aggregation of RBCs has not been studied. Hemolysis (or haemolysis) is defined as the rupture of red blood cells which occurs due to cell membrane damage, resulting in the release of hemoglobin. Deformability is defined as the ability of RBCs to change shape in response to an external force, without rupturing. When human RBCs undergo large deformations under external stresses, they can pass through capillaries that are narrower than the diameter of a resting RBC (Kim et al., 2015). RBCs under low shear rate or at stasis have a unique phenomenon that forms rouleaux formation similar to a stack of coins. RBC aggregation is the main determinant of blood viscosity and sedimentation rate at low shear rate (Shin et al., 2009). Cai et al. (Cai et al., 2015) improved the hemolytic effect of graphene oxide nanosheets on RBCs by grafting bovine serum albumin (GO-g-BSA), where the whole blood was collected from a healthy rabbit. The BSA-modified GO-treated RBCs had typical biconcave shapes, while most of the GO-treated RBCs showed significant alterations in their morphologies, such as echinocytic or acanthocytic shapes. Chowdhury et al. (Chowdhury et al., 2013) also investigated the morphology and hemolysis of whole human blood after treatment with dextran-functionalized graphene nanoplatelets (GNP-Dex), which results from reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation. Liao et al. (Liao et al., 2011) evaluated the hemolysis of human RBCs incubated with various concentrations of graphene oxide (GO) and graphene sheets (GS). Their results showed that GO, with the smallest size is higher hemolyitic than aggregated graphene sheets. However, GO coated with chitosan showed significantly the lowest hemolytic activity. These results suggest that particle size, particulate state, and oxygen content of graphene are main contributors to hemorheological responses to RBCs.

The purpose of our study was to investigate the hemorheological alterations in human RBCs exposed to various concentrations of three GQDs (non-functionalized, hydroxylated, and carboxylated) with varying exposure times. We experimentally measured the hemolysis, deformability, and aggregation of the RBCs exposed to GQDs and observed the RBC shapes using a scanning electron microscope. The novelty of this study lies in the introduction of a hemorheological approach to evaluate GQD toxicity on RBCs. To the best of our knowledge, there are no previous quantitative studies on GQD toxicity on RBCs using hemorheological methods. Our study shows that toxicity evaluations of various nanomaterials can be performed by investigating the hemorheological properties of RBCs exposed to the said nanomaterials.

The non-functionalized GQDs were synthesized as described previously with some modifications (Nurunnabi et al., 2013a). First, 100 mg of carbon fiber was dissolved in 40 mL mixture of acid solutions (sulfuric acid: nitric acid = 3:1). The carbon fiber-containing acid solution was then subjected to bath sonication for 1 h, followed by ultrasonication for 10 min. The mixture was then placed in a 3-neck round bottom flask, which was placed on a heating mantle at 90 °C with stirring for 12 h; this was followed by exposure to N2 gas. After completion of the reaction, the required amount of distilled water was added. Sodium hydroxide and sodium carbonate were added in the specified amounts to make it neutralized (pH 7). The solution was slowly stirred at 0–4 °C for a certain period to remove the precipitated salts. Non-functionalized GQDs were then collected by decantation and freeze-dried for 48 h.

We prepared the hydroxylated GQDs using a previous method with some modifications (Nurunnabi et al., 2013b). First, we prepared GQDs through exfoliation of carbon fibers in a strong acidic mixture for 12 h at 95 ± 5 °C. Simultaneously, polydopamine was formed from dopamine hydrochloride through vigorous stirring at pH 8.5 for 3 h. Next, specific amounts of both GQDs and polydopamine were dissolved in PBS separately and mixed together properly. The mixture was then vortexed for 10 min, followed by incubation for 1 h at room temperature.

The carboxylated GQDs were synthesized from carbon fiber as described in a previous report (Nurunnabi et al., 2013c). In brief, 100 g of carbon fiber was added to 40 mL of sulfuric acid, and the solution was sonicated for 1 h at room temperature. The solution was then injected slowly into a mixture of nitric acid (20 mL) and sulfuric acid (450 mL) at 95 °C. The reaction was carried out for 12 h to exfoliate carbon fiber into monolayer graphene to make GQDs. To introduce carboxyl groups on the surface of the GQDs, citric acid was added, and stirring was continued for 1 h at 60 °C. After completion of the reaction, an excess amount of water was added to the solution. Sodium hydroxide and sodium carbonate were then added to the solution to bring the pH to 8. Next, the solution was placed in an ice bath, and the temperature was maintained at 0–4 °C with slow stirring. The precipitate was then separated from the solution by decantation. The final product was freeze-dried for 3 days to obtain a fine powder.

The UV absorbance of the GQDs with different functional groups was measured by UV spectroscopy (Mecasys. Co. Ltd, South Korea) using a 1 cm-wide quartz cuvette. The fluorescence intensity of the GQDs with the different functional groups was measured by photoluminescence spectroscopy (Sinco, South Korea). The hydrodynamic size of the GQDs was measured by Dynamic light scattering (DLS) at a fixed scattering angle of 175° at 25 °C using a Malvern Instruments Zetasizer Nano S 90 (ZEN1690) (UK) equipped with a 633 nm He-Ne gas laser.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of blood samples and GQD toxicity assessment
ce:section.ce:para: Human venous blood was drawn from three healthy male donors using 21G butterfly infusion into a Vacutainer® (BD, Franklin Lakes, NJ, USA) containing K2-EDTA as the anticoagulant; the donors were volunteers who had not taken any medicine over the previous week. The Kyung Hee University Hospital Committee approved this study and the use of human blood (KMC IRB No: 161604). All measurements for human subject protocol review and biohazard control were followed (Baskurt et al., 2009). Whole blood was centrifuged at the speed of 100 g for 15 min. The plasma and buffy coat and the top layer of cells were discarded from the rest of the RBCs. The plasma was then collected and placed into a 15-mL conical tube for measuring RBC aggregation. The remaining packed RBCs were washed with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) and centrifuged at 300 g for 5 min. After washing twice, the packed RBCs with a volume of 500 μL were diluted to 1.5 mL using PBS (GIBCO®, Life Technologies, NY, USA) at a hematocrit of 25%. Next, 0.3 mL of the diluted RBC suspension was mixed with the GQD dispersions in PBS (1.2 mL) at various concentrations of the GQDs: 0, 500, 750, and 1000 μg/mL. The final hematocrit was fixed at 5%, and 1.2 mL of RBCs suspended in PBS without GQDs was adopted as the control sample. The final samples were gently mixed using a roller mixer and incubated at room temperature. The samples were incubated for 3 h, and the RBC deformability and aggregation was measured every hour.

Free hemoglobin released from the RBCs was measured to evaluate the hemolytic activities of the GQDs to which human RBCs were exposed. The RBC suspensions with different concentrations of three GQDs samples were incubated for 2 h at room temperature. After incubation, the samples were centrifuged at 1700 g for 5 min to spin down the RBCs and GQDs for measurement of hemoglobin release. The absorbance of free hemoglobin released from the RBCs was measured using a spectrophotometer (Synergy™ HT, BioTek®, USA) at a wavelength of 540 nm.

We measured the deformability of the RBCs exposed to the GQDs using a commercial microchip-based ektacytometer (Rheoscan-AnD300; RheoMeditech, Seoul, Korea) at room temperature. The RBC samples incubated with the GQDs were centrifuged at 1700 g for 5 min and washed two times with PBS. Six microliters of the packed RBCs were then mixed with 0.5 mL of a highly viscous polyvinylpyrrolidinone (PVP) solution. The RBC suspension was then injected into a disposable test chip (K-02; RheoMeditech). Test samples were driven by pressure difference between the inlet and outlet of the disposable test chip, which was generated using a piston pump. During this measurement, a beam from a 1.5-mW laser diode with a wavelength of 635 nm was passed through the test sample. The scattered light formed the diffraction images from a diffraction pattern of RBCs on a semitransparent screen, and these images were captured using a charge-coupled device (CCD) video camera at various shear stresses. The captured images of the RBCs were analyzed to calculate the elongation index (EI), which was defined as (L – W)/(L + W), where W and L are the maximum and minimum axes of the ellipse, respectively. Further technical details of RBC aggregation measurements are described in the study by Shin et al. (Shin et al., 2007).

The RBCs incubated with GQDs were washed twice and resuspended by adding plasma. The aggregation of the RBCs exposed to the GQDs was then measured using the light-transmitted, microchip-stirring ektacytometer (Rheoscan-AnD 300, RheoMeditech, Seoul, Korea). Ten microliters of a test sample was loaded into the inlet port of the microchip (C-01, RheoMeditech, Seoul, Korea), which consisted of an air outlet and a stirrer. The stirrer in the microchip started to rotate at the speed of 1000 rpm for 10 s, which caused rapid disaggregation of the RBCs. The stirrer was then suddenly stopped, which caused the RBCs to aggregate during 120 s. RBC aggregation index (AI) was then measured from the plot of the transmitted light intensity versus time. Further technical description of RBC aggregation measurements is provided in the study by Shin et al. (Shin et al., 2009).

The RBCs incubated with different concentrations of GQDs for 3 h were washed twice with PBS and isolated by centrifugation. The RBCs were then diluted using PBS with a mixing ratio of 1:10, dropped on a glass coverslip, and fixed in 2.5% glutaraldehyde (Sigma Aldrich) for 30 min. The RBCs were then dehydrated by exposing them to gradually increasing concentrations of ethanol for 10 min at each step. The dried RBC pellet on the glass coverslip was then sputtered with platinum, and the appearance of the RBCs was observed using a field-emission scanning electron microscope (HITACHI S-4700 FESEM, JAPAN).

All data were analyzed using Statistical Package for Social Sciences Software (SPSS) version 22 (IBM Software, New York, NY, USA). Data were reported as means ± standard deviations (SDs). Statistical testing was performed using analysis of variance (ANOVA), and the statistical significance was represented using p-values. Differences with p-values < 0.05 were considered statistically significant.

Pathak et al. (Pathak et al., 2014) reported that hemolytic activity does not depend on elapsed time because hemolysis is completed within 5 min. Nevertheless, we measured the hemolysis of the RBCs after 2 h of exposure to different concentrations of the non-functionalized, hydroxylated, and carboxylated GQDs. The absorbance of free hemoglobin released from the RBCs through the PBS solution was measured using a spectrophotometer as shown in Fig. 2 . The average hemoglobin absorbance gradually increased as the concentration of the GQDs increased. Overall, our results showed that the hemolytic activity increased gradually with the concentration of the GQDs. The increases in the absorbance of hemoglobin released from the RBCs exposed to the hydroxylated and carboxylated GQDs were higher than that of the non-functionalized GQDs at a concentration of 1000 μg/mL. The average absorbance of hemoglobin at a concentration of 750 μg/mL was 0.073 and 0.065, respectively, which was 21% and 7.2% greater than that of the control sample (0.06). The average absorbance of hemoglobin at a concentration of 1000 μg/mL was 0.744 and 3.117, respectively, which was 1133.7% and 5066.9% greater than that of the control sample (0.06). Based on our results, the toxicity was found to decrease in the order of carboxylated GQDs > hydroxylated GQDs > non-functionalized GQDs, where the carboxylated GQDs seemed to be most detrimental to the RBCs. We used 0, 500, and 750 μg/mL of the GQDs to investigate the deformability and aggregation of the RBCs, since a concentration limit for the GQDs does not cause apparent changes in the hemorheological properties of RBCs.

Fig. 3 shows the diffraction images captured from a diffraction pattern of the RBCs using a charge-coupled device (CCD) video camera at a shear stress of 3 Pa after 3 h of exposure to the GQD nanomaterials (NMs). As the concentration of GQDs increased, the shape of the RBCs became more spherical rather than elliptical. Our results showed that the RBCs exposed to the carboxylated GQDs at a concentration of 750 μg/mL had a spherical shape, and the carboxylated GQDs were found to be most hemolytic towards RBCs among the three GQDs.

We measured the deformability of the RBCs with different exposure times to the GQDs using an ektacytometer (Rheoscan-AnD300; RheoMeditech, Seoul, Korea). The EI representing the RBC deformability was calculated from the diffraction images of the RBCs at a shear stress of 3 Pa from Fig. 3. Overall, the EI values of the RBCs incubated with different concentrations of the GQDs were lower than that of the control sample, as shown in Fig. 4 A. The EI values at a concentration of 750 μg/mL were in the order of non-functionalized, hydroxylated, and carboxylated GQDs, where the carboxylated GQDs were found to show the least deformation among all the GQDs, thus causing the greatest hemolysis and causing the RBCs to become more spherical-shaped (Figs. 2 and 3). The EI values of the RBCs at a concentration of 750 μg/mL were 0.309, 0.268, and 0.043, respectively, which were 3%, 16%, and 86% greater than that of the control sample (0.319). Fig. 4B shows changes in the EI of the RBCs measured for various incubation times. Overall, the EI values for the non-functionalized and hydroxylated GQDs decreased gradually with incubation times while the EI value for the carboxylated GQDs at the incubation time of 1 h obviously decreased. These data indicated that the carboxylated GQDs caused more alterations in the RBC deformability than did the other GQDs.

Fig. 5 shows alterations in the aggregation index of the RBCs measured for various concentrations and incubation times of GQDs. Fig. 5A shows the AI values of the RBCs exposed to GQDs at different concentrations for 3 h. Overall, the AI decreased with increasing GQD concentration. The AI of the RBCs exposed to the non-functionalized GQDs gradually decreased at 500 μg/mL, but no significant differences were observed. However, exposure of the RBCS to the hydroxylated and carboxylated GQDs resulted in significant differences in their AIs. The AIs of the RBCs were greatly decreased when exposed to GQDs at a concentration of 750 μg/mL (77% (non-functionalized), 85% (hydroxylated), and 85% (carboxylated) lower than that of the control sample, 31.6). Fig. 5B shows the AIs of the RBCs exposed to the three GQDs for different times (0, 1, 2, 3, or 4 h) at a concentration of 750 μg/mL. Overall, the AI values of the RBCs exposed to three GQDs greatly decreased with increasing incubation times, but there was little change in the AI value for the control sample. The AIs of the RBCs exposed to the carboxylated GQDs were lower than those of the RBCs exposed to the non-functionalized or hydroxylated GQDS for each incubation time. These data indicate that the carboxylated GQDs caused more alterations in RBC aggregation than did the non-functionalized or hydroxylated GQDs.

We also observed the morphologies of the RBCS exposed to 750 μg/mL of three GQDs for 3 h through SEM images. Fig. 6 A shows that the RBCS in the control sample had a normal biconcave disc shape. When exposed to the non-functionalized GQDs at 750 μg/mL, the RBCs appeared to consist of echinocytes and were typically shaped (Fig. 6B). When exposed to the hydroxylated GQDs, cell-cell merge was observed (Fig. 6C). The RBCs exposed to the carboxylated GQDs became much more damaged than those exposed to the non-functionalized or hydroxylated GQDs, which is in good agreement with the results of the hemolytic activity of the GQDs (Fig. 6D).

Several in vitro studies have been conducted to investigate the hemolysis, deformability, and aggregation of human RBCs exposed to NMs. Kim and Shin (Kim and Shin, 2014) reported that a significant rheological alteration occurs when RBCs are exposed to a concentration of 400 μg/mL of silver nanoparticles with ∼100 nm diameter (d) or 500 μg/mL of silver nanowires with ∼l–2 μm length ( ℓ ) due to hemolysis. In addition, Kim et al. (Kim et al., 2016) investigated the rheological properties of RBCs exposed to various concentrations of silica NMs for varying durations; the silica NMs included silica nanoparticles (d = 200 nm) and silica nanowires (d = 200 nm,
ℓ = 1
μm
or 10 μm). The safe concentration for silica NMs to avoid severe hemolytic activity was found to be less than 12.5 μg/mL. Heo et al. (Heo et al., 2016) studied the toxicity of two different single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWNTs): individual SWNTs and bundled SWNTs and found that bundled SWNTs show higher hemolytic activity than individual SWNTs at a concentration of 0.1 μg/mL. The cytotoxicity study by Khan et al. (Khan et al., 2015) demonstrated that hemolysis in human erythrocytes exposed to zinc oxide or titanium dioxide nanoparticles (NPs) significantly occurred at a concentration of ∼25 μg/mL. The zinc oxide NPs were more toxic than the titanium dioxide ones at higher concentrations. Our present study showed that surface-modified GQDs show insignificant hemolytic activity up to concentrations of 500 μg/mL (Fig. 2), indicating that GQDs exhibit markedly lower cytotoxicity than other NMs such as silica or carbon nanotubes. This is also consistent with the results reported by Chong et al. (Chong et al., 2014) who showed that GQDs have low cytotoxicity because of their ultra-small size and high oxygen content. Additionally, their biocompatibility is supposed to be similar to that of silver nanomaterials.

Although GQDs are less toxic at higher concentrations than the other nanomaterials mentioned above, it is not clear as to why carboxylated GQDs are more toxic than non-functionalized or hydroxylated GQDs and cause hemorheological alterations in RBCs, such as hemolysis, deformability, aggregation, and morphological distortion. Cytotoxicity is known to decrease after NM surface modification (Guo and Mei, 2014); therefore, functionalization could increase cytotoxicity at higher concentrations of NMs owing to problems of purification (as shown in Fig. 4 of the report by Liao et al.) (Liao et al., 2011). Many researchers have reported that cytotoxicity depends on the size, aspect ratio, surface chemistry, ROS, and/or physiological conditions of NMs.

The effects of NM size and aspect ratio on the hemorheological changes in RBCs have been investigated in several studies. The degree of toxicity may arise from the size of the NMs, which causes different levels of endocytosis (Heo et al., 2016; Kim et al., 2016; Kim and Shin, 2014; Rodriguez-Yañez et al., 2013). In our study, the degrees of hemolytic activity of the different GQDs could be correlated with the effective size of the GQDs measured at a concentration of 500 or 750 μg/mL (Table 1), but other factors such as surface chemistry and ROS may also cause rheological changes in RBCs at concentrations >750 μg/mL.

The surfaces of the GQDs in our study were modified with polymer to introduce COOH or OH functional groups, which are hydrophilic. The results of hemolysis measurements showed that carboxylated GQDs were much more hemolytic than the hydroxylated ones at a concentration of 1000 μg/mL. Most functionalized graphene-family nanomaterials tend to aggregate because of electrostatic charges, interaction and nonspecific binding to proteins. Carboxyl graphene increases the release of lactase dehydrogenase (LDH) and the production of ROS, resulting in an increase in cytotoxicity (Guo and Mei, 2014). Low concentrations of carboxylated GQDs show no toxicity on human hepatocellular carcinoma cells, but high concentrations of carboxylated GQDs show toxicity on RBCs because GQDs with a high carboxyl ratio and additional ethanoic acid groups cause a dose-dependent decrease in fluorescent intensity, which indicates plasma membrane damage (Lammel et al., 2013). In addition, purification problems in GQD synthesis may also add to this effect. We synthesized the carboxylated functionalized GQDs using citric acid; some free citric acid remaining along with the carboxylated functionalized GQDs could cause cell membrane damage at high concentrations (Guimarães et al., 2010). After acid treatment, some sulfonation groups are functionalized with GQDs, which come from sulfuric acid (Kanbur and Küçükyavuz, 2011). Corr et al. (Corr et al., 2013) reported that sulfonation groups in graphene materials would be toxic to cells. Despite this, carboxylated GQDs may be biocompatible for in vivo physiological situations because they have been shown to cause no obvious organ damage in KB tumor-bearing mice treated with the GQDs at 5 mg/kg or 10 mg/kg dosages (Nurunnabi et al., 2013c). They reported no toxicity of carboxylated GQDs at 500 μg/mL to three types of cancer cells and normal cells. This similar trend was observed for our studies. We observed no hemolytic activity at 500 μg/mL.

Lastly, another possible reason for the high toxicity of the carboxylated GQDs could be the generation of radical oxidative stress. GQDs move into the cytosol through the cell membrane and are accumulated in vesicles, which results in an increase in intracellular radical oxidative stress levels and membrane damage (Chong et al., 2014). Khan et al. (Khan et al., 2015) showed that radical oxidative species could be generated in vivo using ZnO and TiO2 NPs and demonstrated that the ZnO NPs were significantly more toxic than the TiO2 NPs at higher concentrations. The rate of generation of radical oxidative species in cells may be different at higher concentrations or incubation times for the hydroxylated and carboxylated GQDs in our study.

To the best of our knowledge, our study is the first to investigate the hemorheological characteristics of human RBCs when exposed to three different forms of GQDs (non-functionalized, hydroxylated, and carboxylated GQDs) at various concentrations and with various incubation times. The present study also demonstrates for the first time the hemolytic activities as well as morphologies of RBCs after treatment with the three kinds of GQDs. Carboxylated GQDs were observed to have more substantial hemolytic activity and cause drastic changes in RBC deformability and aggregation than the non-functionalized or hydrophilic GQDs. Therefore, the present study indicates that carboxylated GQDs are more toxic to RBCs than the other two types of GQDs. Our findings indicate that hemorheological measurements can be utilized as tools for risk assessments with regard to NM toxicity to supplement to existing toxicology tests and may provide useful information for nanomaterial safety sheets for NPs.
The authors declare no financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Technology Innovation Program (No. 10054687) funded by the Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy (MI, Korea).

ce:section.ce:para: Transparency document related to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.fct.2016.09.034.
